zotero.abstractNote: Differentiating through optimization layers is a key component of modern machine learning, enabling the integration of structured constraints and physical models into neural networks. Existing approaches often rely on implicit differentiation, which requires solving a linear system at each backward pass, or unrolling, which is memory-intensive. We propose Lagrangian Proximal Gradient Descent (LPGD), a general framework for backpropagation through embedded optimization layers. LPGD treats the optimization process as a sequence of proximal gradient steps and derives the gradient of the solution with respect to the parameters by differentiating through the proximal operator. This approach is memory-efficient, avoids solving linear systems, and is applicable to a wide range of optimization problems, including those with non-smooth objectives and constraints. We demonstrate the effectiveness of LPGD on several tasks, including constrained optimization, model predictive control, and bilevel optimization, showing that it achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods while being more memory-efficient and easier to implement.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning
